stimulus: In many cities, ride-share vehicles often double-park while waiting for or picking up passengers, blocking travel lanes and causing congestion behind them. To address this, transportation researcher Aisha Olufemi proposes that cities install dedicated curbside pick-up zones on busy commercial streets, arguing that requiring ride-share vehicles to use them would substantially reduce traffic congestion and, as a result, lower average commute times in those areas.
stem: Which finding, if true, would most directly support the researcher’s claim?

answerOptions.content: A survey in one city found that seven out of ten respondents felt that ride-share vehicles cause too much congestion.

After a city installed dedicated curbside pick-up zones on its busiest commercial streets and required ride-share vehicles to use them, GPS data showed that average travel times along those streets dropped by about 15%.
A traffic study found that drivers on streets with double-parked ride-share vehicles tend to slow down more often than drivers on streets without them.
In a town that built a single ride-share waiting lot off the main commercial avenue, taxis reported being unable to access the new facility easily.

rationale: Choice B is the best answer because it presents direct evidence that the researcher’s proposed policy works: a real city installed the zones, required ride-share vehicles to use them, and travel times on those streets fell substantially. That straightforwardly supports the claim that the policy reduces congestion and lowers commute times. Choice A reports opinion. Choice C describes a problem but not the policy’s effect. Choice D describes a different policy and a negative outcome for a different group.
